annotations.label: Algebraic variety (classical)

anchor.snippet: an algebraic variety is defined as the set of solutions of a system of polynomial equations

annotations.note: The solution set of polynomials is formalized as `MvPolynomial.zeroLocus`, but no bundled `Variety` type collects this with irreducibility.

annotations.label: Irreducible variety / algebraic set

anchor.snippet: some definitions require an algebraic variety to be irreducible

annotations.note: Irreducibility of zero loci is characterized for the prime spectrum but not for a dedicated classical-variety type.

annotations.label: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz correspondence

anchor.snippet: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz provides a fundamental correspondence between ideals of polynomial rings and algebraic sets

annotations.note: The strong Nullstellensatz `I(Z(I)) = radical I` giving the ideal-algebraic set correspondence is proved directly.

annotations.label: Variety as integral scheme

anchor.snippet: an algebraic variety over a field is an integral (irreducible and reduced) scheme over that field

annotations.note: Integral (= irreducible + reduced) schemes are formalized, but there is no bundled notion of a variety as an integral scheme over a fixed field.

annotations.label: Zero-locus Z(S)

anchor.snippet: define the zero-locus Z ( S ) to be the set of points in A n on which the functions in S simultaneously vanish

annotations.note: `MvPolynomial.zeroLocus` is exactly the set of points where all polynomials of an ideal vanish, with `zeroLocus_span` covering an arbitrary set `S`.

annotations.label: Affine algebraic set

anchor.snippet: A subset V of A n is called an affine algebraic set if V = Z ( S ) for some S

annotations.note: Zero loci are defined, but there is no named predicate `IsAffineAlgebraicSet` for subsets of the form `Z(S)`.

annotations.label: Irreducible affine algebraic set

anchor.snippet: A nonempty affine algebraic set V is called irreducible if it cannot be written as the union of two proper algebraic subsets

annotations.note: Irreducibility of a zero locus is characterized on the prime spectrum via `IsIrreducible`, not on classical affine algebraic sets.

annotations.label: Affine variety

anchor.snippet: An irreducible affine algebraic set is also called an affine variety

annotations.note: Affine schemes (`IsAffine`, schemes isomorphic to `Spec`) exist, but an affine variety as an irreducible affine algebraic set is not bundled.

annotations.label: Zariski topology (affine)

anchor.snippet: Affine varieties can be given a natural topology by declaring the closed sets to be precisely the affine algebraic sets

annotations.note: The Zariski topology with closed sets the zero loci is formalized on the prime spectrum rather than on classical affine n-space.

annotations.label: Ideal I(V)

anchor.snippet: we define I ( V ) to be the ideal of all polynomial functions vanishing on V

annotations.note: `MvPolynomial.vanishingIdeal` is exactly the ideal of polynomials vanishing on a given set of points.

annotations.label: Coordinate ring

anchor.snippet: the coordinate ring or structure ring of V is the quotient of the polynomial ring by this ideal

annotations.note: A coordinate ring is defined only for the special case of an affine Weierstrass curve; the general `k[x]/I(V)` construction is not a named definition.

annotations.label: Zero-locus of homogeneous polynomials

anchor.snippet: define the zero-locus of S to be the set of points in P n on which the functions in S vanish

annotations.note: `ProjectiveSpectrum.zeroLocus` is the set of homogeneous primes containing `S`, the scheme-theoretic projective zero locus.

annotations.label: Projective algebraic set

anchor.snippet: A subset V of P n is called a projective algebraic set if V = Z ( S ) for some S

annotations.note: Projective zero loci exist but there is no named predicate for subsets of the form `Z(S)`.

annotations.label: Projective variety

anchor.snippet: An irreducible projective algebraic set is called a projective variety

annotations.note: The Proj construction and its topology exist, but an irreducible projective algebraic set as a bundled projective variety is not defined.

annotations.label: Zariski topology (projective)

anchor.snippet: Projective varieties are also equipped with the Zariski topology by declaring all algebraic sets to be closed

annotations.note: The Zariski topology on the projective spectrum with closed sets the zero loci is formalized.

annotations.label: Projective coordinate ring

anchor.snippet: let I ( V ) be the ideal generated by all homogeneous polynomials vanishing on V

annotations.note: The homogeneous vanishing ideal is formalized, but the homogeneous coordinate ring quotient is not a named definition.

annotations.label: Quasi-projective variety

anchor.snippet: A quasi-projective variety is a Zariski open subset of a projective variety

annotations.note: There is no notion of quasi-projective variety in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Classical variety (quasi-projective)

anchor.snippet: all varieties were by definition quasi-projective varieties

annotations.note: The historical classical (quasi-projective) variety notion is not formalized.

annotations.label: Abstract algebraic variety (Grothendieck)

anchor.snippet: an abstract algebraic variety is usually defined to be an integral , separated scheme of finite type over an algebraically closed field

annotations.note: All ingredients (integral via `IsIntegral`, separated, locally of finite type) exist as scheme/morphism properties, but no bundled `Variety` type combines them over an algebraically closed field.

annotations.label: Nagata's non-quasiprojective variety

anchor.section: Existence of non-quasiprojective abstract algebraic varieties
anchor.snippet: One of the earliest examples of a non-quasiprojective algebraic variety were given by Nagata

annotations.note: Nagata's example of a non-quasiprojective variety is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Subvariety

anchor.snippet: A subvariety is a subset of a variety that is itself a variety

annotations.note: Closed subschemes via closed immersions and ideal sheaves formalize subvarieties at the scheme level, but not the classical 'subset that is itself a variety'.

annotations.label: Nullstellensatz for closed subvarieties

anchor.snippet: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz says that closed subvarieties of an affine or projective variety are in one-to-one correspondence

annotations.note: The correspondence between irreducible closed subsets and prime ideals is formalized for the prime spectrum.

annotations.label: Line in the affine plane

anchor.snippet: This is called a line in the affine plane

annotations.note: The specific 'line in the affine plane' example is not formalized as a variety.

annotations.label: Unit circle

anchor.snippet: As g ( x , y ) is an absolutely irreducible polynomial, this is an algebraic variety

annotations.note: The unit circle as the variety of an irreducible polynomial is not formalized.

annotations.label: Twisted cubic

anchor.snippet: an algebraic curve that is not contained in any plane

annotations.note: The twisted cubic example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General linear group as affine variety

anchor.snippet: It is an affine variety, since, in general, the complement of a hypersurface in an affine variety is affine

annotations.note: `GL_n` exists as a matrix group but is not endowed with an affine variety/scheme structure.

annotations.label: Algebraic torus

anchor.snippet: A finite product of it ( k × ) r is an algebraic torus , which is again an affine variety

annotations.note: No algebraic torus (as a variety/group scheme) is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear algebraic group

anchor.snippet: A general linear group is an example of a linear algebraic group , an affine variety that has a structure of a group

annotations.note: Group-scheme infrastructure over `Spec` exists, but a dedicated 'linear (affine) algebraic group' notion is not bundled.

annotations.label: Characteristic variety

anchor.snippet: the locus where gr M does not vanish is called the characteristic variety of M

annotations.note: The characteristic variety of a D-module/filtered module is not formalized.

annotations.label: Projective variety (closed subvariety)

anchor.snippet: A projective variety is a closed subvariety of a projective space

annotations.note: Closed immersions into projective space (Proj) can express this, but a bundled projective variety as a closed subvariety of `P^n` is not defined.

annotations.label: Plane projective curve

anchor.snippet: A plane projective curve is the zero locus of an irreducible homogeneous polynomial in three indeterminates

annotations.note: There is no general definition of a plane projective curve as the zero locus of an irreducible homogeneous polynomial.

annotations.label: Elliptic curve

anchor.snippet: which defines a curve in P 2 called an elliptic curve

annotations.note: Elliptic curves are formalized as Weierstrass curves with unit discriminant via the `IsElliptic` typeclass.

annotations.label: Grassmannian variety

anchor.snippet: The Grassmannian variety G n ( V ) is the set of all n -dimensional subspaces of V

annotations.note: `Module.Grassmannian` formalizes the Grassmannian as a set (quotient convention) with a functor, but its representability as a variety/scheme is still a TODO.

annotations.label: Jacobian variety

anchor.snippet: The Jacobian variety Jac( C ) of C is the kernel of this degree map

annotations.note: The Jacobian variety of a curve is not formalized (Mathlib's `Jacobian` files are about Jacobian coordinates of elliptic curves).

annotations.label: Abelian variety

anchor.snippet: an abelian variety , a complete variety with a compatible abelian group structure on it

annotations.note: The key theorem that proper geometrically integral group schemes are commutative is proved, but there is no bundled `AbelianVariety` definition.

annotations.label: Elliptic curve is an abelian variety

anchor.snippet: in particular, an elliptic curve is an abelian variety

annotations.note: The abelian group structure on the points of an elliptic curve is formalized (`AddCommGroup W.Point`), but not phrased as 'is an abelian variety'.

annotations.label: Moduli of curves of genus g

anchor.snippet: the set of isomorphism classes of smooth complete curves of genus g is called the moduli of curves of genus

annotations.note: The moduli space of curves of genus g is not formalized.

annotations.label: Stable curve

anchor.snippet: a stable curve of genus g ≥ 2 , a not-necessarily-smooth complete curve

annotations.note: The notion of a stable curve is not formalized.

annotations.label: Moduli of stable curves

anchor.snippet: the set of isomorphism classes of stable curves of genus g ≥ 2 , is then a projective variety

annotations.note: The moduli of stable curves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Moduli of semistable vector bundles

anchor.snippet: The moduli of semistable vector bundles of a given rank n and a given degree d

annotations.note: Moduli of semistable vector bundles is not formalized.

annotations.label: P1 × A1 (non-affine, non-projective)

anchor.snippet: Here X is an algebraic variety since it is a product of varieties

annotations.note: This specific product variety example is not formalized, though products of schemes exist.

annotations.label: Affine plane minus origin

anchor.snippet: Another example of a non-affine non-projective variety is X = A 2 − (0, 0)

annotations.note: The punctured affine plane example is not formalized as a variety.

annotations.label: Complement of a circle (non-example)

anchor.snippet: is not an algebraic variety (nor even an algebraic set)

annotations.note: This non-example (complement of a circle) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Unitary group vs special linear group

anchor.snippet: a unitary group (over the complex numbers) is not an algebraic variety, while the special linear group SL n ( C ) is a closed subvariety

annotations.note: `SpecialLinearGroup` and `unitaryGroup` exist as matrix groups, but their (non-)variety structures are not formalized.

annotations.label: Variety iff coordinate ring is integral domain

anchor.snippet: An affine algebraic set V is a variety if and only if I ( V ) is a prime ideal

annotations.note: Irreducibility iff prime vanishing ideal is proved for the prime spectrum, the scheme-theoretic analogue of this statement.

annotations.label: Unique decomposition into varieties

anchor.snippet: Every nonempty affine algebraic set may be written uniquely as a finite union of algebraic varieties

annotations.note: Decomposition of closed sets into finitely many irreducible components in a Noetherian space generalizes this; uniqueness is via finiteness of `irreducibleComponents`.

annotations.label: Product of varieties is a variety

anchor.snippet: A product of finitely many algebraic varieties (over an algebraically closed field) is an algebraic variety

annotations.note: Fiber products of schemes exist and integrality of products over a field is studied via geometric integrality, but no single 'product of varieties is a variety' statement is bundled.

annotations.label: Isomorphism of varieties

anchor.snippet: We say V 1 and V 2 are isomorphic , and write V 1 ≅ V 2 , if there are regular maps

annotations.note: Isomorphisms of schemes (categorical `≅` of `Scheme.Hom`) generalize isomorphisms of varieties via regular maps.

annotations.label: Scheme

anchor.snippet: A scheme is a locally ringed space such that every point has a neighbourhood that, as a locally ringed space, is isomorphic to a spectrum of a ring

annotations.note: `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme` is exactly a locally ringed space locally isomorphic to `Spec R`.

annotations.label: Complete variety

anchor.snippet: A complete variety is a variety such that any map from an open subset of a nonsingular curve into it can be extended uniquely to the whole curve

annotations.note: Completeness is captured by properness of a morphism (`IsProper`), with the curve-extension characterization corresponding to the valuative criterion.

annotations.label: Projective implies complete

anchor.snippet: Every projective variety is complete, but not vice versa

annotations.note: `Proj 𝒜` is shown to be proper over `Spec 𝒜₀` (an `IsProper` instance), the scheme-theoretic form of 'projective implies complete'.

annotations.label: Algebraic manifold

anchor.snippet: An algebraic manifold is an algebraic variety that is also an m -dimensional manifold

annotations.note: The notion of algebraic manifold is not formalized.

annotations.label: Nash manifold

anchor.snippet: algebraic manifolds are called Nash manifolds

annotations.note: Nash manifolds are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Riemann sphere

anchor.snippet: The Riemann sphere is one example

annotations.note: The Riemann sphere as an algebraic manifold is not formalized (only the one-point compactification / projective line exist separately).
